mission_goal: Identify and demonstrate a broad set of OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities in the Juice Shop instance, with emphasis on injection flaws, broken authentication, and sensitive data exposure — establishing a comprehensive baseline of exploitable surface area for this target.

known_high_value_paths: /ftp/ — open directory with sensitive files (KeePass .kdbx, package.json.bak)
/encryptionkeys/ — exposes JWT RS256 public key (algorithm confusion attack surface) and premium unlock keys
/support/logs — live access logs and audit.json leaking internal IP and route data
/b2b/v2 — JWT-authenticated API endpoint, target for token forgery
/graphql — GraphQL endpoint, warrants introspection query during enumeration
/rest and /api — primary REST attack surface for SQLi and IDOR
known_behaviors: CORS wildcard (*) on all HTTP methods — amplifies XSS impact significantly
JWT signing uses RS256 with a discoverable public key — HS256 algorithm confusion viable

phase_1_reconnaissance.label: Passive Recon
phase_1_reconnaissance.objective: Fingerprint the target and map publicly exposed endpoints without triggering rate limiting or WAF rules
techniques: HTTP banner grabbing (Server, X-Powered-By, X-Frame-Options headers)
Crawl application to discover JS source bundles — Juice Shop embeds all routes in main.js
Technology fingerprinting via Wappalyzer-equivalent header analysis (Node.js/Express stack)
phase_1_reconnaissance.exit_criteria: Target tech stack confirmed, base URL inventory complete, JS route map extracted

phase_2_scanning.label: Vulnerability Scanning
phase_2_scanning.objective: Automated surface scan to identify low-hanging fruit and prioritize manual exploitation queue
techniques: Nikto or equivalent lightweight HTTP scanner against http://10.10.30.130
Directory brute-force (gobuster/ffuf) against known Juice Shop paths: /ftp/, /encryptionkeys/, /support/logs, /b2b/, /assets/
HTTP method enumeration (OPTIONS) on REST endpoints
GraphQL endpoint detection and initial introspection query at /graphql
phase_2_scanning.exit_criteria: Open directories confirmed, exposed file inventory documented, GraphQL schema retrieved

phase_3_enumeration.label: Deep Enumeration
phase_3_enumeration.objective: Extract actionable data from confirmed exposed surfaces before active exploitation
techniques: Retrieve /ftp/ directory contents — target .kdbx file and package.json.bak for version intel
Download /encryptionkeys/ artifacts — extract JWT public key, note RS256 algorithm in use
Parse /support/logs audit.json for internal route and user activity data
REST API enumeration: probe /rest/user/whoami, /api/Users/, /api/Products/ for IDOR surface
GraphQL introspection: dump full schema, identify mutations (e.g., createUser, updateUser)
phase_3_enumeration.exit_criteria: JWT public key retrieved, user API IDOR surface confirmed, GraphQL schema fully documented

phase_4_exploitation.label: Active Exploitation
phase_4_exploitation.objective: Demonstrate and document up to max_exploits (5) distinct vulnerabilities with reproduction steps

exploit_candidates.technique: Classic OR-based SQLi in email field: ' OR 1=1-- to authenticate as first user (admin)
exploit_candidates.success_indicator: 200 response with authentication token and admin email in response body

exploit_candidates.technique: Retrieve RS256 public key from /encryptionkeys/; re-sign a manipulated token using the public key as the HMAC-SHA256 secret with alg:HS256; escalate role to admin
exploit_candidates.success_indicator: Server accepts forged token, /rest/user/whoami returns admin identity

exploit_candidates.vulnerability: Sensitive Data Exposure via Open Directory
exploit_candidates.technique: Direct browse to open /ftp/ directory; retrieve acquisition.md, eastere.gg, and .kdbx file without authentication
exploit_candidates.success_indicator: Files downloaded; sensitive content (internal docs, credential store) confirmed accessible
exploit_candidates.owasp_category: A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures / A01:2021 Broken Access Control

exploit_candidates.vulnerability: IDOR — Access Any User's Data via REST API
exploit_candidates.technique: Enumerate /api/Users/1 through /api/Users/20 without admin token; observe which user records are returned to unauthenticated or low-privilege requests
exploit_candidates.success_indicator: User PII (email, password hash) returned for IDs other than the authenticated caller's own record

exploit_candidates.technique: Inject <iframe src="javascript:alert('xss')"> or <script>alert(1)</script> variants into the q parameter; CORS wildcard amplifies exploitability
exploit_candidates.success_indicator: Payload renders unescaped in the HTTP response body or executes in browser context

phase_4_exploitation.exit_criteria: All 5 exploit candidates attempted; findings documented with request/response evidence for each confirmed exploit

phase_5_post_exploitation.label: Post-Exploitation
phase_5_post_exploitation.objective: Demonstrate impact of successful exploits — no destructive actions, no persistent changes to lab state
techniques: Using admin JWT from EXP-01 or EXP-02: access /rest/admin/application-configuration to retrieve internal app config
Enumerate all registered users via /api/Users/ with admin token — document credential hash exposure
Retrieve /support/logs/audit.json with admin credentials — document what operational data is exposed
Demonstrate privilege boundary: confirm non-admin endpoints accessible with forged token from EXP-02
constraints: Do NOT modify, delete, or corrupt any user records
Do NOT change admin credentials or application configuration values
Do NOT submit orders or execute financial transactions
Do NOT trigger DoS conditions via fuzzing rate
phase_5_post_exploitation.exit_criteria: Impact scope documented; admin-only data confirmed accessible; no lab state modified

phase_6_reporting.label: Findings Documentation
phase_6_reporting.objective: Produce a structured findings report consumable by redteam-master and the trainee
required_outputs: findings.json — machine-readable list of confirmed exploits with OWASP category, severity, reproduction steps, and request/response evidence
remediation_notes.md — per-finding fix recommendations keyed to exploit IDs (EXP-01 through EXP-05)
phase_6_reporting.exit_criteria: All three output files written to missions/10.10.30.130/; mission_plan.json status updated to 'completed'

learning_objectives: Identify and exploit SQL injection in an authentication endpoint and understand why parameterized queries prevent it
Understand JWT algorithm confusion attacks — specifically how a discoverable RS256 public key enables HS256 token forgery
Recognize and exploit open directory listings as a sensitive data exposure vector
Demonstrate IDOR via REST API enumeration and articulate the difference between authentication and authorization failures
Execute and document reflected XSS, and explain how CORS wildcard policy amplifies client-side injection impact
out_of_scope: Denial of Service or application crash induction
Credential brute-force against external or upstream services
Lateral movement to other network hosts
Modifying or deleting application data in any persistent way
success_criteria: Mission complete when: (a) all 5 exploit candidates in phase_4 have been attempted AND (b) at least 4 of 5 are confirmed with documented request/response evidence AND (c) findings.json, executive_summary.md, and remediation_notes.md are written to missions/10.10.30.130/. Early termination is acceptable if all allowed_attack_types are exhausted before 5 exploits are confirmed.
notes: Lab environment only. Target is OWASP Juice Shop — an intentionally vulnerable Node.js/Express application. All exploits are against synthetic data. The JWT key retrieval from /encryptionkeys/ is a prerequisite for EXP-02 and should be completed during phase_3 before exploitation begins. The CORS wildcard on this target means any XSS finding should be documented with its cross-origin amplification impact noted. Juice Shop resets to clean state on container restart — no persistent lab damage concern.
